claim: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open protocol for connecting AI applications to external tools and data sources.
MCP defines a client-server architecture.
In the MCP client-server architecture, a host application runs one or more MCP clients.
Each MCP client is connected to an MCP server that exposes tools, resources, and prompts.
MCP servers commonly communicate over two transports.
MCP servers commonly use stdio for local subprocess servers.
MCP servers commonly use HTTP-based transports for remote servers.
The protocol specifies a capability-negotiation handshake during initialization.
The capability-negotiation handshake lets a client discover which features a server offers.
The features a server may offer include tools, resources, prompts, and sampling.
Adoption of MCP has grown across multiple AI coding and agent platforms.
Several major vendors are shipping MCP client support.
It is widely believed that MCP will become the dominant standard for agent-tool interoperability within a year.
Competing approaches to MCP remain.
Some observers speculate that nearly every enterprise agent deployment will rely on MCP by 2027.
It is uncertain whether nearly every enterprise agent deployment will rely on MCP by 2027.
A typical integration exposes domain capabilities as tools with JSON-schema inputs.
A typical integration returns structured results the host model can reason over.
Servers may expose resources as read-only context.
Servers may expose reusable prompt templates.
Ollama is a tool for running open-weight language models locally.
Ollama exposes an HTTP API on port 11434 by default.
Ollama provides an OpenAI-compatible endpoint at `/v1`.
Existing OpenAI-style clients can target a local model by changing the base URL.
Running models locally has zero marginal cost per request once the hardware is owned.
Running models locally is attractive for high-volume or background workloads.
Latency depends on the model size and the GPU.
Quality depends on the model size and the GPU.
Large models on consumer GPUs can be slow.
Quantized variants trade some quality for speed.
Quantized variants trade some quality for lower memory use.
It is sometimes claimed that local open-weight models now match frontier hosted models on most tasks.
On hard reasoning the performance gap between local open-weight models and frontier hosted models generally remains.
On long-form synthesis the performance gap between local open-weight models and frontier hosted models generally remains.
Local models are often sufficient for summarization.
Local models are often sufficient for extraction.
Local models are often sufficient for draft synthesis.
Ollama can list installed models via its API.
Ollama can pull new models on demand.
Ollama can serve multiple models from one instance.
Whether a given local model is good enough for a task should be decided by evaluation.
One should not assume a local model is good enough for a task simply because it is free.
Auto-mode routing selects a model per query based on task complexity and a quality/cost tradeoff.
Simple factual lookups can be served by small, cheap models.
Complex synthesis and deep research route to stronger, more expensive models.
Routing quality depends on the data behind it.
With measured benchmark rankings, routing can pick the best model per task type.
Without benchmarks, provisional rankings are used.
Deriving provisional quality from price alone is unreliable.
Cheap models often perform near frontier on easy tasks.
Published benchmark priors give better provisional routing than price.
A reported figure suggests cost savings of 10-20x from routing simple queries to budget models instead of always using a flagship.
The exact cost savings figure depends on the query mix.
Some claim auto-routing eliminates the need for any manual model selection.
Operators frequently still pin a model for reproducibility.
The cheapest-capable principle is to pick the least expensive model whose measured quality clears a task's floor.
The cheapest-capable principle requires honest quality estimates.
The cheapest-capable principle requires a value-aware selection step.
The cheapest-capable principle does not rely on quality-maximization alone.
